title: Matrix phase-space representations for quantum symmetries
authors: Peter D. Drummond, Alexander S. Dellios, Margaret D. Reid
abstract: We introduce a general phase-space representation that includes global quantum symmetries in the basis expansion. This method, called matrix phase-space, projects the basis onto a reduced Hilbert space, which can greatly reduce sampling errors of many-body quantum simulations and unifies several previous phase-space methods. The purpose of this paper is to provide detailed proofs of basic theorems and operator identities. We also treat several different types of symmetries. To illustrate the benefits of matrix phase-space methods, we give a detailed derivation of a recent application to the topical problem of verifying the outputs of Gaussian boson sampling (GBS) quantum computers with photon number resolving detectors. This has exponential complexity, and using parity symmetry reduces sampling errors by very large factors relative to earlier methods.
summary: The paper introduces the novel 'matrix phase-space' formalism to simulate complex many-body quantum dynamics by incorporating global quantum symmetries directly into the state representation. This method projects the system onto a reduced Hilbert space, leading to significantly lower sampling errors in simulations of systems like Gaussian Boson Sampling. It provides essential mathematical identities for mapping quantum evolution equations into solvable forms.
main_problem: The core problem is developing a mathematically rigorous, general phase-space representation that can accurately simulate large-scale many-body quantum dynamics while explicitly incorporating global quantum symmetries.
main_result: The matrix phase-space method provides an expansion that projects the density matrix onto a reduced Hilbert space defined by symmetry projectors, significantly reducing sampling errors compared to standard methods like Wigner or Q-functions.
method:
The authors introduce the 'matrix phase-space' representation, which generalizes existing phase-space techniques by encoding symmetries ($\hat{\Pi}_{
u}$) directly into the basis expansion and kernel operator. This allows for deriving necessary operator identities mapping Liouville/master equations to generalized Fokker-Planck Equations.
model_or_system: The theory is applied to general bosonic quantum systems, with specific detailed applications shown for Gaussian Boson Sampling (GBS) quantum computers and analyzing the dynamics of squeezed vacuum states in lossy environments. The formalism handles both discrete symmetries (like parity) and continuous symmetry limits.
key_observables: Total photo-count distribution; sampling errors/difference errors between simulated and exact distributions; expectation values of photon number ($\langle \hat{n}_j angle$).
important_parameters: Squeezing parameter ($r$); loss rate (e.g., $\approx 3\%$); symmetry projection indices ($	ilde{p}$); the limit $M 	o \infty$ for continuum approximations.
main_assumptions: The method assumes that symmetries can be effectively captured by commuting projectors, and derivations often rely on specific factorizations of the kernel operator and vanishing boundary terms when transforming dynamics into FPEs.
figures_summary: Figures compare the matrix-P distribution against exact results for GBS (lossless and lossy), demonstrating superior accuracy in reducing sampling errors compared to standard positive-P methods, especially in low-photon-number regimes.
paper_structure: The paper builds from general theory by defining the matrix phase-space expansion using symmetry projectors. It then derives fundamental operator identities for this representation (e.g., action of $\hat{a}_k$ on projected states). Finally, it validates the method through detailed applications to GBS simulations in both ideal and lossy settings.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it provides a powerful theoretical tool—the matrix-P representation—to tackle the exponential complexity of simulating quantum systems with symmetries, which is crucial for understanding near-term photonic quantum computing devices like GBS.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Upper plot: Matrix-P simulations of the total photo-count probability (solid blue lines) versus the exact multi-mode squeezed state photon counting distribution for GBS (dashed black lines) for squeezed states with uniform squeezing parameter r = [0.75, . . . , 0.75] and a Haar random unitary matrix of size M = 300. Numerical probabilities are obtained by averaging over ensembles of size ES = 1.2 × 106. Lower plot: Difference errors of the exact and matrix-P simulated distributions, which are so small that they are not visible.

Figure 3. Upper plot: Comparison of the matrix-P (solid blue line), positive-P (dash dot red line) and exact lossy (dashed black line) total photo-count distribution for the network in Fig.(1) with ≈3% uniform photon loss. Both the positive-P and matrix-P distributions converge to the exact case in this regime. Bottom plots: Typical difference errors (showing lines for ±1σ) of the matrix-P (left plot) and positive-P (right plot) simulated and exact distributions.

Figure 2. Description follows Fig.(1) except now the positive-P phase-space distribution is used for simulations of the total photo-count probability (solid red lines). Differ- ences are ≈103 times larger than matrix-P and are clearly visible. These are caused by a skewed distribution requiring enormous sample numbers to reach full convergence.
